I made another thread to illustrate the k-shot prompting method mentioned above:

Repeated sampling from instruction-tuned models can yield low-diversity, stereotyped output. For creative tasks, k-shot prompting base models will improve creativity (measured e.g. as vocabulary size), though generally with worse adherence to the (now implicit) instructions.

Note in the final image: - Our prompt ends with “Output:” — constraining the model’s response to contain only the output we want - After our desired response, the base model continues to produce more input/output pairs (only one shown) — avoiding this requires a stop sequence.

Instruction-tuned LLMs can suffer from limited diversity. More creative base models don’t take orders at all. Luckily, an instruct LLM (like Claude 3.5 Sonnet) can turn your instructions into equivalent k-shot examples for prompting a base model (like Llama 3.1 405B base):
Generically, an IF model could access a base model as a tool — with enough training to translate requests to pre-instruct prompts — it could maybe learn to be creative while also staying sane.

“Calibrated Language Models Must Hallucinate” — Hallucination rate from a pre-trained LLM ≈ proportion of facts seen only once in training. RLHF reduces hallucination but makes LLMs less well calibrated as models. Real text has news; LLMs need to unlearn that fact to please us.
